title: चन्द्रघण्टा स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री चन्द्रघण्टा स्तोत्रम् ॥

वन्दे वाञ्छितलाभाय चन्द्रार्धकृतशेखराम्।
सिंहारूढां दशभुजां चन्द्रघण्टां यशस्विनीम्॥१॥

कञ्चनाभां मणिपुरस्थितां तृतीय दुर्गां त्रिनेत्राम्।
खड्गादिभिर्दशभुजां चन्द्रघण्टां प्रपूजयेत्॥२॥

पटाम्बरपरिधानां मृदुहास्या नानालंकारभूषिताम्।
मञ्जीरहारकेयूरकिंकिणीरत्नकुण्डलम्॥३॥

प्रफुल्लवदनां पल्लवाधरां कान्तकपोलां तुंगकुचाम्।
कमनीयां लावण्यां क्षीणकटिं हारनूपुरशोभिताम्॥४॥

तृतीया दुर्गा त्वंहि भवसागरतारिणी।
धन ऐश्वर्य दायिनी चन्द्रघण्टे प्रणमाम्यहम्॥५॥

त्रिलोकपालिनी त्वंहि सद्गति दायिनी शुभे।
पराक्रमदायिनी चन्द्रघण्टे प्रणमाम्यहम्॥६॥

चन्द्रमुखी इष्टदात्री इष्टमन्त्रस्वरूपिणी।
धनदात्री आनन्ददात्री चन्द्रघण्टे प्रणमाम्यहम्॥७॥

नानारूपधारिणी इच्छामयी ऐश्वर्यदायिनी।
सौभाग्यारोग्यदायिनी चन्द्रघण्टे प्रणमाम्यहम्॥८॥

असुरवधकारिणी सिंहारूढा दशभुजा।
मणिपुरनिवासिनी चन्द्रघण्टे प्रणमाम्यहम्॥९॥

घण्टामधुरनादिनी कञ्चनतनू मनोहरा।
नवदुर्गासु तृतीया चन्द्रघण्टे नमोऽस्तु ते॥१०॥

॥ इति श्री चन्द्रघण्टा स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Chandraghanta Stotram ||

Vande Vanchhita-labhaya Chandrardhakrita-shekharam.
Simharudham Dashabhujam Chandraghantam Yashasvinim.||1||

Kanchanabham Manipura-sthitam Tritiya Durgam Trinetram.
Khadgadibhir Dashabhujam Chandraghantam Prapujayet.||2||

Patambara-paridhanam Mridu-hasya Nana-alankara-bhushitam.
Manjira-hara-keyura-kinkini-ratna-kundalam.||3||

Praphulla-vadanam Pallava-adharam Kanta-kapolam Tunga-kucham.
Kamaniyam Lavanyam Kshina-katim Hara-nupura-shobhitam.||4||

Tritiya Durga Tvamhi Bhava-sagara-tarinim.
Dhana Aishwarya Dayini Chandraghante Pranamamyaham.||5||

Triloka-palini Tvamhi Sadgati Dayini Shubhe.
Parakrama-dayini Chandraghante Pranamamyaham.||6||

Chandra-mukhi Ishta-datri Ishta-mantra-svarupini.
Dhana-datri Ananda-datri Chandraghante Pranamamyaham.||7||

Nana-rupa-dharini Ichchha-mayi Aishwarya-dayini.
Saubhagya-arogya-dayini Chandraghante Pranamamyaham.||8||

Asura-vadha-karini Simharudha Dashabhuja.
Manipura-nivasini Chandraghante Pranamamyaham.||9||

Ghanta-madhura-nadini Kanchana-tanu Manohara.
Navadurgasu Tritiya Chandraghante Namo'stu Te.||10||

|| Iti Shri Chandraghanta Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
वाञ्छित फल प्राप्ति के लिए अर्धचन्द्र से शेखरा, सिंह पर आरूढ, दशभुजा, यशस्विनी चन्द्रघण्टा की वन्दना करता हूँ।

काञ्चन (स्वर्ण) कान्ति वाली, मणिपुर चक्र में स्थित, तृतीय दुर्गा, त्रिनेत्रा, खड्ग आदि दश आयुधों वाली चन्द्रघण्टा की पूजा करें।

हे तृतीय दुर्गा! आप भवसागर तारिणी, धन-ऐश्वर्य दायिनी हैं। आप त्रिलोकपालिनी, सद्गतिदायिनी, पराक्रमदायिनी हैं।

चन्द्रमुखी, इष्टदात्री, धनदात्री, आनन्ददात्री, अनेक रूप धारिणी, इच्छामयी, ऐश्वर्यदायिनी, सौभाग्य-आरोग्यदायिनी।

असुरों का वध करने वाली, सिंह पर आरूढ, दशभुजा, मणिपुर में निवास करने वाली, घण्टा की मधुर ध्वनि करने वाली — नवदुर्गा में तृतीय चन्द्रघण्टा को नमस्कार।
benefits: Invokes the third Navdurga form for Navratri Day 3 worship
Activates the Manipura (solar plexus) chakra for courage and power
Invokes the blessing of valour, fearlessness, and victory over enemies
Traditionally recited for removal of sorrow and grants eternal happiness (ananda)
description: Dedicated to Goddess Durga, the Chandraghanta Stotram (चन्द्रघण्टा स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. Drawn from Traditional (Navdurga Stotras), this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to invoke the third Navdurga form for Navratri Day 3 worship as well as grant saubhagya (good fortune) and arogya (health). Additionally, it is known to grant wealth, prosperity, and aiswarya (lordship). This prayer is traditionally recited on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and Friday (Shukravar) and during Navratri to invoke divine grace. At 10 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 5 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Chandraghanta Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Chandraghanta Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Prayer to third Navdurga form for Navratri Day 3 for courage and prosperity.

seoFaqs.question: Why is the Devi in this stotram called 'Chandraghanta'?
seoFaqs.answer: The name Chandraghanta is a compound of chandra (moon) and ghanta (bell). It refers to the half-moon shaped like a bell that the Devi wears on her forehead, a distinguishing iconographic feature recorded in Devi Bhagavata and reproduced in the Navadurga sequence followed by Drik Panchang and other liturgical sources. The stotram opens with this very epithet because the bell-shaped crescent is said to emit a continuous resonance that frightens demonic forces and dispels negativity around the worshipper. Iconographically, this is also the form in which Parvati first turns into a warrior after her marriage to Shiva, which is why the hymn pairs imagery of the crescent with imagery of weapons in her ten hands.

seoFaqs.question: Where does the Chandraghanta Stotram fit in the Navratri sequence?
seoFaqs.answer: Chandraghanta is the third of the Navadurgas and is therefore worshipped on the third day of Sharad Navratri, after Shailaputri (day 1) and Brahmacharini (day 2). Liturgical calendars such as Drik Panchang assign her a specific tithi color and prescribe her stotram, dhyana shloka and beeja mantra for that day's sankalpa. The Navadurga Stotram itself contains a dedicated verse for Chandraghanta beginning 'Pindaja-pravararudha chandakopastra-kairyuta', which is often recited in expanded form as the Chandraghanta Stotram during the morning puja. Devotees who undertake the full nine-night vrata typically chant her stotram 3, 9 or 108 times on day three before moving to Kushmanda's worship on day four.

seoFaqs.question: What does the principal verse 'Pindaja-pravararudha' actually mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The verse 'Pindaja-pravararudha chandakopastra-kairyuta, prasadam tanute mahyam chandraghanteti vishruta' translates roughly as: 'Mounted on a mighty lion, bearing fearsome weapons drawn in fierce wrath, may she who is renowned as Chandraghanta bestow her grace upon me.' Pindaja-pravara refers to the lion (born of the lineage of pinda), chandakopa-astra-kairyuta describes her ten arms holding weapons drawn in righteous anger against adharma, and prasadam tanute is the petition for her favour. Commentators including those at JKYog and Kirtan Chalisa note that the verse compresses three theological points into one shloka: her warrior aspect, her grace toward devotees, and her established fame (vishruta) under the Chandraghanta name.

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does tradition attribute to chanting the Chandraghanta Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional phala-shruti material associated with the stotram emphasises courage, removal of fear and dispelling of negative astral influences. Because the Devi's bell-shaped crescent is said to vibrate continuously, devotees who chant her stotram are believed to develop mental steadiness, clarity of speech and protection against unseen obstacles such as evil eye and recurring nightmares. Sources such as Rudraksha Ratna and harishyam.com summarise the expected fruits as inner discipline, peace in the household and protection during travel. Practically, householders chant the stotram during Sharad Navratri for family welfare, while sadhakas may add it to their daily Devi parayana for cultivation of veerya (valour) and confident speech.

seoFaqs.question: What should one do before reciting the Chandraghanta Stotram on day three of Navratri?
seoFaqs.answer: On the third day of Navratri, the practitioner is advised to bathe, wear clean clothes (traditionally in the day's prescribed colour as listed by Drik Panchang), and set up the Devi puja with kalasha, lamp and the day's specific naivedya, often kheer or milk-based offerings since Chandraghanta is associated with the moon. The sankalpa is taken naming the day, tithi and the specific intention, after which dhyana of her ten-armed lion-mounted form is performed. The stotram is then recited 3, 9, 21 or 108 times, followed by aarti and prarthana for forgiveness of mistakes in the recitation. Most pandits recommend keeping the recitation aloud but unhurried, since the bell-symbolism of the Devi rewards rhythmic, resonant chanting.

seoFaqs.question: Is Chandraghanta the same goddess as Chandika or Chamunda?
seoFaqs.answer: No, the three are related but distinct in Shakta iconography. Chandraghanta is specifically the third Navadurga, the post-marriage warrior form of Parvati distinguished by the crescent-bell on her forehead and a benevolent expression even when armed. Chandika is a broader epithet of Durga used throughout the Devi Mahatmya for the goddess in her battle aspect, while Chamunda is the fierce emanation born from Kali's brow to slay Chanda and Munda. Devotees occasionally conflate them because all three appear in lion-mounted, multi-armed warrior form, but the Chandraghanta Stotram addresses only the Navadurga sequence form and does not invoke the cremation-ground imagery associated with Chamunda.
